design_philosophy.core_loop: Pilot the LEO-04 Rynex fighter through horizontally-scrolling stages with vertical exploration, acquiring weapons from defeated enemies, and adapting weapon selection to boss weaknesses
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward of vertical exploration for hidden power-ups versus maintaining safe horizontal flight path; weapon cycling decisiveness during boss encounters
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite fighter pilot commanding an advanced warship against an alien empire, progressively unlocking devastating weaponry including the mythic Thunder Sword
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasizes player agency in movement speed and vertical exploration; refuses to mandate a single optimal weapon, instead requiring tactical adaptation. Stage order flexibility (first 4 stages selectable) respects player choice within a directed narrative. Graphics and environmental scale are design priorities, with parallax scrolling serving both aesthetic and navigation purposes.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard arcade-style lives system (specific count undocumented)
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpoints; no mid-stage checkpointing documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can adjust ship speed at any time for maneuverability; Saturn port introduced an easier mode with bonus defensive abilities, indicating difficulty scaling was later considered

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon drop system: defeated enemies drop power-ups that arm ship with missiles and lasers. Each weapon has distinct firing patterns and situational effectiveness. Player can hold multiple weapons and cycle between them. Satellite ships multiply firepower and are required (×2) to unlock Thunder Sword attack.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Thunder Sword unlocks mid-game upon collecting 2 satellite ships; represents progression milestone. Different weapons unlock via enemy drops rather than story gatekeeping.
replayability_hooks: Selectable stage order for first 4 stages encourages route experimentation
Hidden power-ups revealed only through vertical exploration reward thorough stage knowledge
Weapon variety creates distinct playstyles per run
Saturn version added secret playable ship from Thunder Force III

world_building_techniques: Explicit narrative framing: player takes role of fighter pilot battling Orn Empire plotting human extinction
Sequel continuation: story occurs 2 years after Thunder Force III events, establishing series continuity
Environmental scale and parallax scrolling create sense of vast alien landscapes and military conflict
Boss encounters (including mid-stage bosses) serve as narrative checkpoints of campaign progress

boss_design.philosophy: Pattern-based encounters requiring weapon adaptation and timing execution. Boss design acknowledges weapon variety by making certain weapons more effective than others per encounter, forcing player decision-making rather than mechanical dominance.
notable_bosses: Darkstar (implied final boss, given North American title 'Quest for the Darkstar')

faction_indicators: Orn Empire as antagonistic faction pursuing human extinction
Human resistance/military as implied player faction
danger_signals: Orn Empire weaponry and ship designs encountered in stages
Mid-stage boss appearances indicating escalating threat levels
Environmental hazards suggesting hostile alien territory
environmental_storytelling: Vertical and parallax scrolling creates perception of vast, layered alien environments under Orn control
Stage design reveals different operational theaters of conflict through visual variety
Enemy wave patterns and distribution suggest military fortification and defensive strategies
